title: Pesto Pasta

subs.name: fresh coriander + a few mint leaves for an Indian-fusion pesto
subs.notes: add a green chilli for heat; the flavour profile is different but delicious

ingredients.name: cashews (substitute for pine nuts)
visual_checks: pine nuts or walnuts are traditional; cashews are widely available in India
ingredients.notes: pine nuts or walnuts are traditional; cashews are widely available in India

subs.name: walnut, almond or actual pine nuts if available
subs.notes: walnuts give a slightly more bitter pesto; blanch almonds first

subs.name: nutritional yeast (3 tbsp) for a vegan pesto
subs.notes: also omit cheese and ensure the pasta water binds the sauce

subs.name: cold-pressed coconut oil or refined sunflower oil
subs.notes: the flavour will be milder; use a light-tasting oil to let the basil shine

ingredients.name: lemon juice
visual_checks: keeps the pesto bright green and adds freshness
ingredients.notes: keeps the pesto bright green and adds freshness

steps.text: Lightly toast the cashews in a dry pan over low heat for 2 minutes until pale golden. Set aside to cool — toasted nuts add depth to the pesto.

Combine basil leaves, cooled cashews, garlic and grated cheese in a blender or mixie. Pulse a few times to break down the ingredients, then blend while drizzling in the olive oil in a slow, steady stream. Blend to a textured paste — not completely smooth. Season with salt, pepper and a squeeze of lemon.
Cook pasta in a large pot of heavily salted boiling water until al dente — follow packet timings. Reserve 150 ml of the starchy pasta water before draining.
Transfer the drained pasta to a large bowl (not on a hot flame). Add 3–4 tablespoons of pasta water to the pesto and stir — this loosens the pesto and helps it coat the pasta evenly.

Pour the pesto over the pasta and toss well, adding more pasta water a tablespoon at a time until every strand is coated in a glossy, bright-green sauce. Do not return the pasta to heat as the pesto will discolour and the cheese will become grainy.
Serve immediately in warm bowls with an extra drizzle of olive oil and a few basil leaves on top.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: basil → fresh coriander + a few mint leaves for an Indian-fusion pesto (add a green chilli for heat; the flavour profile is different but delicious); cashew → walnut, almond or actual pine nuts if available (walnuts give a slightly more bitter pesto; blanch almonds first); olive-oil → cold-pressed coconut oil or refined sunflower oil (the flavour will be milder; use a light-tasting oil to let the basil shine); cheese → nutritional yeast (3 tbsp) for a vegan pesto (also omit cheese and ensure the pasta water binds the sauce)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A standard Indian mixie blends pesto in under a minute — use the small chutney jar for best results. Fresh basil is available at supermarkets like More, D-Mart and Nature's Basket in major cities; hydroponic basil is now found in Swiggy Instamart. Cashews are an excellent and cheaper substitute for pine nuts (which are expensive imports). Extra-light olive oil is adequate here; the flavour is subtler but pesto is still distinctive.
